raw.college_scorecard.description: Program-level career outcomes for bachelor's degree programs at U.S. postsecondary institutions, sourced from the U.S. Department of Education College Scorecard. Each row represents one bachelor's degree program at one institution, with median earnings, median student debt, and program completion counts. This is the foundational dataset for FutureProof's career guidance analysis.
raw.college_scorecard.source: U.S. Department of Education College Scorecard — Most Recent Cohorts Field of Study

unitid.description: The unique 6-digit identifier assigned to every U.S. postsecondary institution by the federal IPEDS system (Integrated Postsecondary Education Data System). This is the authoritative way to identify a specific college or university, and it remains stable across years. For example, Stanford University is always 110635. This field is part of the table's grain — every row must have a unitid.
unitid.cde_rationale: Grain field. Required for entity resolution, CIP-to-SOC crosswalk joins in Silver zone, and all downstream aggregations. Incorrect values would orphan rows from institutional context.
unitid.notes: All values are positive 6-digit integers in the range 100,654 to 497,268. 2,559 distinct institutions in the current load.

instnm.description: The official name of the institution (college or university) as reported to the federal IPEDS system. This is the human-readable label for the institution — for example, 'Stanford University' or 'University of Phoenix'. Note that 10 institution names appear for multiple campuses (e.g., Stevens-Henager College has 6 campus entries with different unitids). The unitid field, not this name, is the authoritative identifier.
instnm.notes: 2,534 distinct names for 2,559 distinct unitids. Each unitid maps to exactly one name, but some names map to multiple unitids (multi-campus systems).

cipcode.description: The Classification of Instructional Programs (CIP) code that identifies what academic program (field of study) this row represents. For example, code '5202' means Business Administration and Management. CIP codes are a federal taxonomy used by all U.S. colleges for reporting. In this raw dataset, codes are stored as 4-digit strings without the standard dot separator (e.g., '5202' instead of '52.02'). The Silver zone will normalize these to standard XX.XXXX format. This field is part of the table's grain.
cipcode.cde_rationale: Grain field. Foundation for the CIP-to-SOC crosswalk in Silver zone, which is the critical join enabling the core FutureProof question: 'If I study X at school Y, what career outcomes can I expect?'
cipcode.notes: 390 distinct CIP codes observed. All are exactly 4 characters. Leading zeros are preserved (e.g., '0100'). Top programs by frequency: 5202 (Business Admin, 1,701 rows), 4201 (Psychology, 1,438), 2601 (Biology, 1,411).

cipdesc.description: The human-readable name of the academic program corresponding to the CIP code. For example, if the cipcode is '5202', the cipdesc is 'Business Administration and Management, General.' This is a display label — the cipcode is the authoritative identifier for the program.
cipdesc.notes: 390 distinct values, in 1:1 correspondence with cipcode. All values end with a period.

creddesc.description: The human-readable description of the credential level. In this MVP dataset, every row has the value 'Bachelor's Degree' because the ingestor filters to bachelor's-level programs only (credlev=3). This field is redundant with credlev in the current dataset but will become meaningful if future loads include other credential levels.
creddesc.notes: Single value: 'Bachelor's Degree' (100% of rows). Redundant with credlev in MVP.

credlev.description: An integer code indicating the credential level of the academic program. In this dataset, every row has the value 3, which means 'Bachelor's Degree.' The ingestor filters the source data to only include bachelor's-level programs. The full code set is: 1=Undergraduate Certificate, 2=Associate's, 3=Bachelor's, 4=Post-baccalaureate Certificate, 5=Master's, 6=Doctoral, 7=First Professional, 8=Graduate Certificate. This field is part of the table's grain.
credlev.cde_rationale: Grain field. Although constant (=3) in this MVP, it is part of the dedup grain and validates that the CREDLEV=3 ingestor filter operated correctly. Future loads may include additional credential levels.
credlev.notes: Constant value of 3 in current load. Part of the dedup grain along with unitid and cipcode.

md_earn_wne.description: Median earnings of graduates who are working and not enrolled in further education. IMPORTANT: This field is 100% null in this dataset. It appears to be an institution-level metric that does not populate in the field-of-study data file. It should be dropped or investigated before Silver zone processing. Do not use this field for any analysis.
md_earn_wne.notes: CRITICAL: 100% null (69,947 of 69,947 rows). This field is structurally empty at the field-of-study grain. Root cause: the source CSV column likely only populates in the institution-level file, not the field-of-study file. Recommend dropping in Silver zone.

earn_mdn_hi_1yr.description: The median earnings (high estimate) of graduates one year after completing this program. This is a cohort-level aggregate, not individual-level data — it represents the typical earnings of all graduates from this specific program at this specific institution who were working and not enrolled in further education, measured one year after completion. Values are in U.S. dollars. Approximately 64% of rows have null values due to Department of Education privacy suppression, which protects student privacy when program cohorts are small (generally fewer than 30 completers).
earn_mdn_hi_1yr.cde_rationale: Primary business metric. Core to the FutureProof value proposition (career outcome guidance). Feeds downstream earnings analysis, program comparison rankings, and potential Gainful Employment ratio calculations.
earn_mdn_hi_1yr.notes: 64.0% null rate due to privacy suppression. Non-null range: $4,880 to $161,723. Median: $35,812. Mean: $39,616. Programs with 30+ completers have 88.7% availability. IMPORTANT: This is NOT the same cohort tracked over time as the 2yr field — the 1yr and 2yr figures come from different cohort measurement windows.

earn_mdn_hi_2yr.description: The median earnings (high estimate) of graduates two years after completing this program. Like the 1-year earnings field, this is a cohort-level aggregate in U.S. dollars. IMPORTANT: The 2-year figure is NOT the same group of people tracked from the 1-year measurement — it comes from a different cohort measurement window. This means comparing 1yr and 2yr values as 'earnings growth' would be misleading. Approximately 60% of rows have null values due to Department of Education privacy suppression.
earn_mdn_hi_2yr.cde_rationale: Primary business metric. Together with 1yr earnings, provides the multi-horizon view of graduate outcomes central to FutureProof career guidance.
earn_mdn_hi_2yr.notes: 60.4% null rate. Non-null range: $5,938 to $160,116. Median: $35,810. Mean: $39,596. Distribution is nearly identical to 1yr. Has 2,485 more non-null values than 1yr field. 44.2% of rows with both values present show 2yr < 1yr — this is expected, not an error.

debt_all_stgp_eval_mdn.description: The median cumulative federal loan debt of students who completed this program, in U.S. dollars. This reflects how much the typical graduate borrowed in federal student loans by the time they finished this specific program at this specific institution. Approximately 63% of rows have null values due to Department of Education privacy suppression for small program cohorts.
debt_all_stgp_eval_mdn.cde_rationale: Primary business metric. Required for debt-to-earnings ratio analysis (related to Gainful Employment rules) and cost-benefit assessment of programs. Central to the student decision-making use case that FutureProof serves.
debt_all_stgp_eval_mdn.notes: 63.1% null rate. Non-null range: $2,750 to $57,500. Median: $23,000. Mean: $22,869. High-debt outliers (>$50k) are concentrated at for-profit institutions.

ipedscount1.description: The number of students who completed this program as reported to the federal IPEDS system, using the first measurement window. This gives a sense of program size — how many graduates the program produces. A value of 0 means the program existed but had no reported completions in the measurement period. Programs with higher completion counts are more likely to have earnings and debt data available (due to privacy suppression thresholds).
ipedscount1.notes: 8.7% null rate. Non-null range: 0 to 7,888. Median: 11. Mean: 33.73 (right-skewed by large online/for-profit programs). 12.4% of non-null rows have value 0. Top programs: Chamberlain Nursing (7,888), U of Phoenix Business (5,615).

ipedscount2.description: The number of students who completed this program as reported to IPEDS, using the second measurement window. This is a parallel count to ipedscount1 from a different reporting period. The two counts are very highly correlated (r=0.984) but not identical.
ipedscount2.notes: 8.3% null rate. Non-null range: 0 to 8,124. Median: 10. Mean: 33.98. Very high correlation with ipedscount1 (r=0.984).

control.description: An integer code indicating the type of governance or ownership of the institution. Values: 1 = Public (state-funded), 2 = Private nonprofit, 3 = Private for-profit. This field was added to the raw ingestor after the initial Bronze load to support the Silver zone institution_control field. It classifies institutions by their funding and ownership structure, which is an important factor in analyzing career outcomes — for-profit institutions often show different earnings and debt patterns than public or nonprofit institutions.
control.notes: Added to raw schema to support Silver zone institution_control derivation. Integer values 1-3 map to text labels in Silver. Source: College Scorecard CONTROL field (column 4 in source CSV).

ingested_at.description: The exact date and time when this row was loaded into the raw zone by the data pipeline. This is pipeline metadata, not source data — it records when the ingestion happened, not when the underlying education data was collected.
ingested_at.notes: Pipeline-generated field. Single value in current load: 2026-04-06 02:34:20.757243. All rows share the same timestamp (single-batch load).

source_url.description: The URL from which this data was downloaded. This is pipeline metadata that records provenance — where the data came from. In the current load, all rows have the same value pointing to the Department of Education's bulk download endpoint.

source_method.description: The method used to acquire this data. In the current pipeline, the value is always 'bulk_csv_download', indicating the data was downloaded as a bulk CSV file from the Department of Education.

load_date.description: The calendar date when this data was loaded into the pipeline. This is pipeline metadata used to track which load a row belongs to, especially useful when multiple loads occur over time.
load_date.notes: Pipeline-generated field. Single value in current load: 2026-04-06.

base.college_scorecard.description: Clean, modeled base table of program-level career outcomes for bachelor's degree programs at U.S. postsecondary institutions. Each row represents one program offering: a specific academic program at a specific institution at a specific credential level. This table is the trusted, query-ready foundation for all downstream Gold zone products including debt-to-earnings ratios, program rankings, and AI-powered career guidance. Compared to the raw zone, CIP codes are normalized to standard XX.XXXX format, columns have business-meaningful names, the structurally empty md_earn_wne field is dropped, and a small_cohort_flag identifies programs where outcome data may be unreliable due to privacy suppression.

record_id.description: A unique identifier for each row, computed automatically from the combination of institution ID, CIP code, and credential level. This ID is stable — running the pipeline again on the same data produces the same ID for the same row. Format: 'cs-' followed by 16 hexadecimal characters (e.g., 'cs-a1b2c3d4e5f67890'). Useful for tracking specific records across pipeline runs.
record_id.source_column: Derived from unitid + cipcode + credlev via compute_grain_id()
record_id.notes: Primary key. Deterministic SHA-256 hash truncated to 16 hex chars with 'cs' prefix.

unitid.description: The unique 6-digit identifier assigned to every U.S. postsecondary institution by the federal IPEDS system. This is the authoritative way to identify a specific college or university, and it remains stable across years. For example, Stanford University is always 110635. This field is part of the table's grain — combined with cipcode and credential_level, it uniquely identifies each row.
unitid.cde_rationale: Grain field. Required for entity resolution, CIP-to-SOC crosswalk joins, and all downstream aggregations. Incorrect values would orphan rows from institutional context.
unitid.notes: Natural key component. 2,559 distinct institutions. Range: 100,654 to 497,268.

institution_name.description: The official name of the institution (college or university) as reported to the federal IPEDS system. For example, 'Stanford University' or 'University of Phoenix'. Note that some institution names appear for multiple campuses with different unitids — the unitid field, not this name, is the authoritative identifier.
institution_name.notes: Renamed from 'instnm' in raw zone for business clarity.

institution_control.description: The type of governance or ownership of the institution. One of three values: 'Public' (state-funded institutions like state universities), 'Private nonprofit' (institutions like most private colleges), or 'Private for-profit' (commercially operated institutions). This classification matters because career outcomes — earnings and debt patterns — vary significantly by institution type.
institution_control.notes: Derived from integer CONTROL field in raw zone. CHECK constraint enforces allowed values. Required for Gold zone segmentation.

cipcode.description: The Classification of Instructional Programs (CIP) code that identifies what academic program this row represents. In this table, codes are in the standard XX.XXXX format (e.g., '52.02' for Business Administration). CIP codes are a federal taxonomy used by all U.S. colleges for reporting. This field is part of the table's grain.
cipcode.notes: Natural key component. Normalized from raw 4-digit format (e.g., '5202' becomes '52.02'). 390 distinct codes.

program_name.description: The human-readable name of the academic program corresponding to its CIP code. For example, 'Business Administration and Management, General.' for CIP code 52.02. This is a display label — the cipcode field is the authoritative program identifier.
program_name.notes: Renamed from 'cipdesc' in raw zone for business clarity. 390 distinct values in 1:1 correspondence with cipcode.

cip_family.description: The broad discipline area that this program belongs to, represented as a 2-digit code. For example, '52' means Business, Management, Marketing. This is derived from the first two characters of the CIP code and is useful for grouping and filtering programs by discipline.
cip_family.notes: 45 distinct CIP families observed. All are valid CIP 2020 taxonomy codes.

cip_family_name.description: The human-readable label for the broad discipline area. For example, 'Business, Management, Marketing, and Related Support Services' for CIP family 52. This is looked up from the official CIP 2020 taxonomy based on the cip_family code.
cip_family_name.source_column: Derived via CIP 2020 taxonomy lookup on cip_family
cip_family_name.notes: 45 distinct family names. Lookup must resolve for all observed 2-digit codes.

credential_level.description: An integer code indicating the type of credential awarded by this program. In this MVP dataset, every row has the value 3, meaning 'Bachelor's Degree'. The full code set is: 1=Undergraduate Certificate, 2=Associate's, 3=Bachelor's, 4=Post-baccalaureate Certificate, 5=Master's, 6=Doctoral, 7=First Professional, 8=Graduate Certificate. IMPORTANT: This is a categorical code, not a number you should average or sum. This field is part of the table's grain.
credential_level.notes: Natural key component. Constant value 3 in MVP. Categorical code — do not aggregate.

credential_description.description: The human-readable label for the credential level. In this dataset, the value is always 'Bachelor's Degree'. This field will become more useful when the pipeline includes additional credential levels beyond bachelor's.
credential_description.notes: Renamed from 'creddesc' in raw zone. Constant 'Bachelor's Degree' in MVP.

earnings_1yr_median.description: The median earnings of graduates one year after completing this program, in U.S. dollars. This is a cohort-level statistic — it represents the typical earnings of all graduates from this specific program at this specific institution who were working and not enrolled in further education. Approximately 64% of rows have null values because the U.S. Department of Education suppresses this data when programs have too few graduates (generally fewer than 30) to protect student privacy. IMPORTANT: The 1-year and 2-year earnings come from different groups of graduates measured at different times, so comparing them as 'earnings growth' would be misleading.
earnings_1yr_median.cde_rationale: Primary business metric. Core to the FutureProof value proposition (career outcome guidance). Feeds downstream earnings analysis, program comparison rankings, and potential Gainful Employment ratio calculations.
earnings_1yr_median.notes: 64.0% null rate. Non-null range: $4,880-$161,723. Renamed from 'earn_mdn_hi_1yr'. Valid range constraint: $1,000-$250,000.

earnings_2yr_median.description: The median earnings of graduates two years after completing this program, in U.S. dollars. Like the 1-year figure, this is a cohort-level statistic. Approximately 60% of rows have null values due to privacy suppression. IMPORTANT: This comes from a different group of graduates than the 1-year figure — it is NOT the same people tracked over time. It is valid for the 2-year number to be lower than the 1-year number.
earnings_2yr_median.cde_rationale: Primary business metric. Together with 1yr earnings, provides the multi-horizon view of graduate outcomes central to FutureProof career guidance.
earnings_2yr_median.notes: 60.4% null rate. Non-null range: $5,938-$160,116. Renamed from 'earn_mdn_hi_2yr'. 44.2% of rows with both values show 2yr < 1yr — expected, not an error.

debt_median.description: The median total federal student loan debt of graduates who completed this program, in U.S. dollars. This represents how much the typical graduate borrowed by the time they finished. Approximately 63% of rows have null values due to privacy suppression. High-debt outliers (over $50,000) tend to be concentrated at for-profit institutions.
debt_median.cde_rationale: Primary business metric. Required for debt-to-earnings ratio analysis (related to Gainful Employment rules) and cost-benefit assessment of programs. Central to the student decision-making use case.
debt_median.notes: 63.1% null rate. Non-null range: $2,750-$57,500. Renamed from 'debt_all_stgp_eval_mdn'. Valid range constraint: $1,000-$100,000.

completions_count_1.description: The number of students who completed this program, as reported to the federal IPEDS system using the first measurement window. This gives a sense of program size — how many graduates the program produces. Programs with higher completion counts are more likely to have earnings and debt data available. A value of 0 means the program existed but had no completions reported. This field drives the small_cohort_flag.
completions_count_1.notes: 8.7% null rate. Non-null range: 0-7,888. Renamed from 'ipedscount1'. Must be non-negative when present.

completions_count_2.description: The number of students who completed this program, as reported to IPEDS using the second measurement window. This is a parallel count to completions_count_1 from a different reporting period. The two counts are very highly correlated (r=0.984) but not identical.
completions_count_2.notes: 8.3% null rate. Non-null range: 0-8,124. Renamed from 'ipedscount2'. Supplementary to completions_count_1.

small_cohort_flag.description: A true/false flag that indicates whether this program has a small number of graduates (fewer than 30 completers). When true, it means the program's earnings and debt data are likely to be suppressed for privacy reasons and any available outcome data should be interpreted with caution. Programs are flagged but NOT removed from the dataset — this lets downstream consumers decide how to handle them. If the completions count is unknown (null), the flag defaults to true as a conservative safety measure.
small_cohort_flag.source_column: Derived from completions_count_1 (True when null or < 30)
small_cohort_flag.notes: 75.52% True, 24.48% False. Conservative default: NULL completions produce True. Approved by human reviewer.

source_load_date.description: The date on which the source data was loaded into the pipeline's raw zone. This tells you when the data was fetched from the Department of Education, not when the underlying education outcomes were measured. Useful for tracking data freshness and identifying which load batch a row belongs to.
source_load_date.notes: Pipeline metadata. Single value in current load: 2026-04-06.

ingested_at.description: The exact date and time when this row was written to the Silver zone base table. This is generated automatically at transformation time and is used for pipeline auditing and tracking data freshness. It tells you when the Silver transformation ran, not when the source data was collected.
ingested_at.notes: Pipeline metadata. Generated at transformation time via datetime.now().

consumable.career_outcomes.description: The core career outcomes data product for FutureProof. Each row represents one academic program at one institution at one credential level, enriched with cross-institution percentile bands, debt-to-earnings ratios, earnings rankings, and data confidence tiers. This table powers the FutureProof query pattern: 'If I study X at school Y, what earnings and debt outcomes can I expect?' All 69,947 Silver base rows are carried forward with flags (not filtered), so downstream consumers can apply their own filtering criteria.

record_id.description: A unique identifier for each row, computed automatically from the combination of institution ID, CIP code, and credential level. This ID is stable across pipeline re-runs. Format: 'co-' followed by 16 hexadecimal characters. The 'co' prefix distinguishes Gold career outcomes records from Silver records (which use 'cs' prefix).
record_id.source_column: Derived from unitid + cipcode + credlev via compute_grain_id(prefix='co')

unitid.description: The unique 6-digit identifier assigned to every U.S. postsecondary institution by the federal IPEDS system. This is the authoritative way to identify a specific college or university. For example, Stanford University is always 243744. Combined with cipcode and credential_level, it uniquely identifies each row in this table.
unitid.cde_rationale: Natural key component identifying the institution. Required for all downstream joins, MCP layer queries, institution-level aggregations, and future CIP-to-SOC crosswalk integration.

institution_name.description: The official name of the institution as reported to the federal IPEDS system. For example, 'Massachusetts Institute of Technology'. The unitid field, not this name, is the authoritative identifier.

institution_control.description: The type of governance or ownership of the institution: 'Public', 'Private nonprofit', or 'Private for-profit'. NOTE: Currently 100% null due to a Bronze re-ingestion blocker (the CONTROL field was not included in the original raw parquet). Tracked by DQ rule GLD-CO-039.
institution_control.notes: Currently null for all rows. Will populate once Bronze zone re-ingests with CONTROL field.

cipcode.description: The Classification of Instructional Programs (CIP) code identifying the academic program, in standard XX.XXXX format (e.g., '11.07' for Computer Science). CIP codes are a federal taxonomy used by all U.S. colleges. This field is part of the table's grain.

program_name.description: The human-readable name of the academic program. For example, 'Computer Science.' for CIP code 11.07. This is a display label; the cipcode field is the authoritative identifier.

cip_family.description: The broad discipline area that this program belongs to, represented as a 2-digit code. For example, '11' means Computer and Information Sciences, '51' means Health Professions, '52' means Business. This field is the partition key for all percentile band and earnings rank calculations.

cip_family_name.description: The human-readable label for the broad discipline area. For example, 'Computer and Information Sciences and Support Services' for CIP family 11.

credential_level.description: An integer code indicating the credential type. In this MVP dataset, every row has the value 3 (Bachelor's Degree). This is a categorical code, not a number you should average or sum. Part of the table's grain.

earnings_1yr_median.description: The median earnings of graduates one year after completing this program, in U.S. dollars. This is a cohort-level statistic representing the typical outcome for all graduates from this program at this institution. About 64% of rows are null because the Department of Education suppresses data when programs have too few graduates to protect student privacy. This field drives 5 downstream derived columns: debt-to-earnings ratio, earnings growth rate, CIP-family earnings rank, program value index, and earnings percentile bands.
earnings_1yr_median.cde_rationale: Primary business metric powering FutureProof's core query pattern. Feeds the effort slider UI, debt-to-earnings ratio, CIP-family earnings rank, program value index, and percentile band derivations. Incorrect values propagate to 5 downstream derived columns.

earnings_2yr_median.description: The median earnings of graduates two years after completing this program, in U.S. dollars. IMPORTANT: This comes from a different group of graduates than the 1-year figure. The 2-year value may be lower than the 1-year value. About 60% of rows are null due to privacy suppression.
earnings_2yr_median.cde_rationale: Primary business metric providing multi-horizon view of graduate outcomes. Feeds earnings growth rate and 2-year percentile band derivations.

debt_median.description: The median total federal student loan debt of graduates who completed this program, in U.S. dollars. About 63% of rows are null due to privacy suppression. High-debt outliers (over $50,000) tend to be concentrated at for-profit institutions.
debt_median.cde_rationale: Primary business metric for student debt exposure. Feeds debt-to-earnings ratio, debt-to-earnings tier, program value index, and debt percentile bands. Incorrect values corrupt 4 downstream derived columns.

completions_count.description: The number of students who completed this program, from the first IPEDS measurement window. Renamed from 'completions_count_1' in Silver for clarity. Drives the small_cohort_flag. A higher count generally means more reliable earnings and debt data.

small_cohort_flag.description: True when the program has fewer than 30 completers or the completions count is unknown. When true, outcome data is likely subject to privacy suppression and should be interpreted with caution. Programs are flagged but NOT removed from the dataset. Feeds into confidence tier derivation.

earnings_1yr_p25.description: The 25th percentile of 1-year median earnings across all institutions in the same CIP family (discipline area). This tells you: 'For programs in this discipline, the bottom-quartile institutional outcome is this amount.' Null if the CIP family has fewer than 3 programs with reported earnings (insufficient data for a reliable distribution). This is NOT a within-cohort percentile of individual earners.
earnings_1yr_p25.cde_rationale: Powers the lower bound of the effort slider UI for 1-year earnings. Directly consumed by the MCP layer and product UI.
earnings_1yr_p25.source_column: Derived via PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) over earnings_1yr_median PARTITION BY cip_family

earnings_1yr_p75.description: The 75th percentile of 1-year median earnings across all institutions in the same CIP family. This tells you: 'For programs in this discipline, the top-quartile institutional outcome is this amount.' Null if fewer than 3 non-null values in the CIP family. Always greater than or equal to earnings_1yr_p25.
earnings_1yr_p75.cde_rationale: Powers the upper bound of the effort slider UI for 1-year earnings. Directly consumed by the MCP layer and product UI.
earnings_1yr_p75.source_column: Derived via PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) over earnings_1yr_median PARTITION BY cip_family

earnings_2yr_p25.description: The 25th percentile of 2-year median earnings across all institutions in the same CIP family. Null if fewer than 3 non-null values.
earnings_2yr_p25.cde_rationale: Provides the lower bound of the 2-year earnings range within CIP family for multi-horizon career guidance.
earnings_2yr_p25.source_column: Derived via PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) over earnings_2yr_median PARTITION BY cip_family

earnings_2yr_p75.description: The 75th percentile of 2-year median earnings across all institutions in the same CIP family. Null if fewer than 3 non-null values. Always greater than or equal to earnings_2yr_p25.
earnings_2yr_p75.cde_rationale: Provides the upper bound of the 2-year earnings range within CIP family for multi-horizon career guidance.
earnings_2yr_p75.source_column: Derived via PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) over earnings_2yr_median PARTITION BY cip_family

debt_p25.description: The 25th percentile of median debt across all institutions in the same CIP family. This tells you the lower bound of typical debt for programs in a given discipline. Null if fewer than 3 non-null values in the CIP family.
debt_p25.cde_rationale: Provides the lower bound of the debt range within CIP family for affordability context.
debt_p25.source_column: Derived via PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) over debt_median PARTITION BY cip_family

debt_p75.description: The 75th percentile of median debt across all institutions in the same CIP family. This tells you the upper bound of typical debt for programs in a given discipline. Null if fewer than 3 non-null values. Always greater than or equal to debt_p25.
debt_p75.cde_rationale: Provides the upper bound of the debt range within CIP family for affordability context.
debt_p75.source_column: Derived via PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) over debt_median PARTITION BY cip_family

debt_to_earnings_annual.description: The ratio of a program's median debt to its median first-year earnings (debt_median / earnings_1yr_median). This is a key affordability metric: values below 1.0 mean debt is less than one year of earnings (manageable); values above 2.0 mean debt significantly exceeds first-year earnings (concerning). Null when either debt or earnings data is missing due to privacy suppression.
debt_to_earnings_annual.cde_rationale: Key affordability metric aligned with Department of Education Gainful Employment rule methodology. Directly consumed by the MCP layer and product UI.
debt_to_earnings_annual.source_column: Derived: debt_median / earnings_1yr_median (null if either input null)

debt_to_earnings_tier.description: A plain-English classification of how manageable a program's debt is relative to earnings. Four tiers: 'Low' (ratio below 0.75 -- debt very manageable), 'Moderate' (0.75 to 1.5 -- typical range), 'High' (1.5 to 2.5 -- may need extended repayment), 'Very High' (2.5 or above -- debt far exceeds first-year earnings). Null when the underlying ratio is null.
debt_to_earnings_tier.source_column: Derived from debt_to_earnings_annual via CASE WHEN bucketing

earnings_growth_rate.description: The relative difference between 2-year and 1-year median earnings, computed as (2yr - 1yr) / 1yr. IMPORTANT: This is NOT tracking the same people over time. The 1-year and 2-year figures come from different graduating cohorts, so negative values are common (about 44% of programs) and expected. Null when either earnings value is missing.

cip_family_earnings_rank.description: How this program's earnings compare to all other programs in the same discipline, on a scale from 0.0 (lowest) to 1.0 (highest). For example, a rank of 0.95 means this program's graduates earn more than 95% of programs in the same field at other institutions. Null when earnings data is missing.
cip_family_earnings_rank.source_column: Derived: PERCENT_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY cip_family ORDER BY earnings_1yr_median)

program_value_index.description: A simple return-on-investment measure: first-year earnings divided by total debt (earnings_1yr_median / debt_median). Higher values mean better value -- more earnings per dollar of debt. For example, a value of 5.0 means graduates earn 5 times their debt in the first year. This is the mathematical inverse of the debt-to-earnings ratio. Null when either earnings or debt data is missing.

confidence_tier.description: A four-level quality rating for how much you should trust this row's outcome data. 'high' means large cohort with both earnings and debt data (most trustworthy). 'medium' means large cohort but only earnings or only debt data. 'low' means small cohort but some data available. 'insufficient' means no earnings or debt data at all. Every row gets a tier -- there are no nulls. About 50-55% of rows are 'insufficient' due to widespread privacy suppression.

has_earnings.description: A convenience flag that is true when the program has any earnings data available (either 1-year or 2-year or both). Makes it easy to filter to programs with outcome data without checking multiple columns.
has_earnings.source_column: Derived: earnings_1yr_median IS NOT NULL OR earnings_2yr_median IS NOT NULL

has_debt.description: A convenience flag that is true when the program has debt data available. Makes it easy to filter to programs with debt information without checking debt_median for null.

outcome_completeness.description: A score from 0.0 to 1.0 showing what fraction of the three core outcome fields (1-year earnings, 2-year earnings, debt) have data for this program. Exactly 0.0 means none are available, 0.33 means one is available, 0.67 means two, and 1.0 means all three. This helps quickly assess how complete a program's outcome profile is.
outcome_completeness.source_column: Derived: count of non-null among (earnings_1yr_median, earnings_2yr_median, debt_median) / 3.0

source_load_date.description: The date when the source data was loaded into the pipeline's raw zone. This tells you when the data was fetched from the Department of Education, not when the education outcomes were measured.

promoted_at.description: The exact date and time when this row was promoted to the Gold zone consumable table. This is pipeline metadata used for auditing and tracking data freshness. It replaces the Silver zone's ingested_at timestamp.
promoted_at.source_column: Generated at Gold promotion time via CURRENT_TIMESTAMP

raw.bls_ooh.description: Occupation-level employment projections, wage data, and education requirements from the Bureau of Labor Statistics Employment Projections program (the structured data behind the Occupational Outlook Handbook). Each row represents one detailed occupation classified by SOC code, with current and projected employment counts, median annual wage, and the typical education, experience, and training needed to enter that occupation. This is the second data source in the FutureProof pipeline — the SOC code in this table is the primary join key for Silver zone integration with College Scorecard (via the CIP-to-SOC crosswalk) and with O*NET (direct SOC join).
raw.bls_ooh.source: Bureau of Labor Statistics, Employment Projections program (Table 1.7 — Occupational projections and worker characteristics, XLSX download)
raw.bls_ooh.record_count_note: Expected ~832 detailed occupations after filtering ~22 summary rows. Current EDA based on 10-row sample.

soc_code.description: The Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) code that uniquely identifies this occupation. SOC is the federal taxonomy used by BLS, O*NET, and the Census Bureau to classify all U.S. occupations. The format is XX-XXXX, where the first two digits identify the major occupation group (for example, 15 = Computer and Mathematical) and the last four digits identify the specific occupation (for example, 15-1252 = Software Developers). This field is the grain of the table — there is exactly one row per SOC code. It is also the primary join key for connecting BLS occupation data to College Scorecard programs via the CIP-to-SOC crosswalk in the Silver zone.
soc_code.cde_rationale: Grain field and primary cross-source join key. Required for CIP-to-SOC crosswalk (linking programs to occupations) and direct join to O*NET task-level data. Incorrect values would break FutureProof's core program-to-career linkage.
soc_code.notes: Coerced to string, preserving XX-XXXX format with hyphen. Summary SOC codes (XX-0000) are filtered out by the ingestor — only detailed occupations appear in this table. SOC 2018 classification is current; watch for SOC 2028 migration.

occupation_title.description: The official name of the occupation as published by the Bureau of Labor Statistics. For example, 'Software developers' or 'Registered nurses.' Each SOC code maps to exactly one title. Some titles include clarifying exclusions, such as 'Surgeons, except ophthalmologists.' This is a human-readable label — the soc_code field is the authoritative identifier for the occupation.
occupation_title.cde_rationale: Required for display in all downstream career guidance products. The occupation name is how end users identify and understand occupations. Feeds Gold zone outputs and MCP grounding documents.
occupation_title.notes: Title case with exceptions for articles and prepositions. 1:1 correspondence with soc_code. Direct string copy from source.

employment_current.description: The number of people currently employed in this occupation across the United States, as of the base year of the BLS projection cycle (2023 in the current release). The source data reports this value in thousands (for example, 1795.5 means approximately 1,795,500 workers), and the ingestor multiplies by 1,000 and rounds to a whole number. This gives a sense of the size of the occupation in the U.S. labor market.
employment_current.notes: Converted from thousands to actual count (value * 1000, rounded to long). Must be strictly positive for detailed occupations. Sample range: 36,600 (Surgeons) to 3,747,500 (Home health aides).

employment_projected.description: The number of people projected to be employed in this occupation at the end of the BLS projection horizon (2033 in the current release). Like employment_current, this value is converted from thousands to actual count. Comparing this to employment_current shows whether BLS expects the occupation to grow or shrink over the projection period.
employment_projected.notes: Converted from thousands to actual count. Must be strictly positive for detailed occupations. Sample range: 38,500 to 4,373,100.

employment_change.description: The numeric change in employment between the base year (2023) and the projected year (2033), in number of workers. A positive value means the occupation is expected to add jobs; a negative value means it is expected to lose jobs. This is simply the difference between projected and current employment, converted from thousands to actual count. For example, a value of 625,600 means BLS expects 625,600 more workers in this occupation by 2033.
employment_change.notes: Converted from thousands to actual count. CAN be negative for declining occupations (unlike employment_current and employment_projected which must be positive). Should equal employment_projected - employment_current within +/- 1000 rounding tolerance.

employment_change_pct.description: The projected percent change in employment between the base year and the projected year. For example, a value of 16.7 means the occupation is expected to grow 16.7% over the projection period. Negative values indicate declining occupations. This is the most commonly cited BLS growth statistic and is central to FutureProof's career outlook analysis — it tells a student how fast or slow a field is growing.
employment_change_pct.cde_rationale: Primary business metric for career outlook. Directly answers the student question 'Is this career growing or shrinking?' Feeds Gold zone career outcomes and MCP grounding documents.
employment_change_pct.notes: Stored as a percentage value (e.g., 16.7 not 0.167). Can be negative for declining occupations. Sample range: 0.9% to 16.7%. Full dataset expected to range roughly -30% to +30%.

openings_annual_avg.description: The estimated average number of job openings per year in this occupation over the projection period. This includes openings from both growth (new positions) and replacement (workers retiring or leaving the occupation). Even occupations that are shrinking overall still have annual openings because existing workers leave. This is a practical measure of how many entry points exist for someone pursuing this career.
openings_annual_avg.notes: Converted from thousands to actual count. Always positive — even declining occupations have replacement openings. Sample range: 2,100 (Surgeons) to 684,100 (Home health aides).

median_annual_wage.description: The median annual wage for workers in this occupation, in U.S. dollars. 'Median' means half of workers in this occupation earn more and half earn less. For most occupations, this is a straightforward dollar amount. However, there are two special cases: (1) For very high-earning occupations where the median exceeds BLS reporting limits, the wage is top-coded at $239,200 — check the median_wage_capped field to know if this applies. (2) For certain occupations where standard wage data is not available (such as elected officials), this field is null.
median_annual_wage.cde_rationale: Primary business metric for career guidance. Directly answers the student question 'How much does this career pay?' Core input to FutureProof's program-to-career value analysis and debt-to-earnings comparisons.
median_annual_wage.notes: Source reports as string with special values. Ingestor parses: '>=239,200' -> 239200.0 with capped flag; 'N/A' -> null. Null expected for ~2-4% of occupations (~20-30 of ~832). Sample range: $33,530 to $239,200 (capped).

median_wage_capped.description: A flag indicating whether the median wage for this occupation is top-coded (capped) by BLS. When true, it means the actual median wage exceeds $239,200 but BLS does not report the exact figure — the median_annual_wage field shows $239,200 as a floor, not the true median. When false, the wage in median_annual_wage is the actual reported median (or null for N/A occupations). Approximately 3-5% of occupations (mostly physicians, executives, and other high-earning professions) are expected to be capped.
median_wage_capped.source_column: Derived from Median Annual Wage string parsing
median_wage_capped.notes: Consistency rule: median_wage_capped=true IFF median_annual_wage=239200.0. If median_annual_wage is null, this must be false. Always required (never null).

education_typical.description: The typical level of education that most workers need to enter this occupation, as classified by BLS. This is a human-readable label such as 'Bachelor's degree', 'High school diploma or equivalent', or 'Doctoral or professional degree'. BLS defines eight education levels, from 'No formal educational credential' (for occupations like farmworkers) up to 'Doctoral or professional degree' (for physicians, lawyers, etc.). This is the entry-level requirement — some workers in the occupation may have more or less education.
education_typical.notes: Maps 1:1 with education_code. Full value set: No formal educational credential, High school diploma or equivalent, Some college no degree, Postsecondary nondegree award, Associate's degree, Bachelor's degree, Master's degree, Doctoral or professional degree.

education_code.description: A numeric code representing the typical entry-level education requirement for this occupation, using the BLS education classification system. The codes are: 1 = No formal educational credential, 2 = High school diploma or equivalent, 3 = Some college no degree, 4 = Postsecondary nondegree award, 5 = Associate's degree, 6 = Bachelor's degree, 7 = Master's degree, 8 = Doctoral or professional degree. Higher numbers generally correspond to more education. This code is useful for filtering and sorting occupations by education level.
education_code.cde_rationale: Required for Silver zone integration to match occupations with appropriate education pathways. Enables filtering career outcomes by education level — critical for FutureProof's program-to-career guidance (e.g., showing only occupations that typically require a bachelor's degree).
education_code.notes: Integer range 1-8. Maps 1:1 with education_typical. Sample covers codes 3, 6, 8 out of the full 1-8 range.

work_experience.description: The typical amount of work experience in a related occupation that is needed to enter this occupation, as classified by BLS. Values are: 'None' (no related experience needed), 'Less than 5 years', or '5 years or more'. For example, management occupations often require 5+ years of experience in the field they manage, while most entry-level technical roles require no prior related experience.
work_experience.source_column: Work Experience in a Related Occupation
work_experience.notes: Maps 1:1 with work_experience_code. Three possible values: None, Less than 5 years, 5 years or more.

work_experience_code.description: A numeric code representing the typical related work experience requirement for this occupation. The codes are: 1 = None, 2 = Less than 5 years, 3 = 5 years or more. This code is useful for filtering occupations by experience requirements.

training_typical.description: The typical on-the-job training that most workers need to attain competency in this occupation, as classified by BLS. Values range from 'None' (for occupations where formal education is sufficient) to specialized training paths like 'Apprenticeship' (for trades like electricians) or 'Internship/residency' (for physicians). This tells a prospective worker what additional training to expect after being hired or after completing their formal education.
training_typical.notes: Maps 1:1 with training_code. Six possible values: None, Short-term on-the-job training, Moderate-term on-the-job training, Long-term on-the-job training, Apprenticeship, Internship/residency.

training_code.description: A numeric code representing the typical on-the-job training requirement for this occupation. The codes are: 1 = None, 2 = Short-term on-the-job training, 3 = Moderate-term on-the-job training, 4 = Long-term on-the-job training, 5 = Apprenticeship, 6 = Internship/residency. This code is useful for filtering and sorting occupations by training requirements.

ingested_at.description: The exact date and time when this row was loaded into the raw zone by the data pipeline. This is pipeline metadata, not source data — it records when the ingestion happened, not when BLS published the projections data.
ingested_at.notes: Pipeline-generated field (framework BaseIngestor). All rows in a single batch share the same timestamp.

source_url.description: The URL from which this data was downloaded. This is pipeline metadata that records provenance — where the data came from. Points to the BLS Employment Projections data tables page or to the local fallback cache path if the HTTP download received a 403 error.
source_url.notes: Pipeline-generated field. Expected value: https://www.bls.gov/emp/tables/occupational-projections-and-characteristics.htm or fallback path data/raw/xlsx_cache/bls_ooh.xlsx.

source_method.description: The method used to acquire this data. In the current pipeline, the value is 'xlsx_download', indicating the data was downloaded as an XLSX file from the Bureau of Labor Statistics website (or read from a local cache if the download was blocked).

load_date.description: The calendar date when this data was loaded into the pipeline, formatted as YYYY-MM-DD. This is pipeline metadata used to track which load a row belongs to, especially useful when the BLS data is refreshed on a new 2-year projection cycle.
load_date.notes: Pipeline-generated field. Freshness rule RAW-OOH-017 checks that load_date is within 30 days.

base.bls_ooh.description: Clean, modeled Silver base table of occupation-level employment projections, wages, and education requirements from the Bureau of Labor Statistics. Each row represents one occupation classified by its Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) code. This table contains 832 occupations from the BLS 2024-2034 projection cycle, with derived classification fields (SOC major group, broad occupation flag, catchall flag, growth category) that prepare the data for downstream Gold zone career outcomes products. The SOC code in this table is the anchor point for the CIP-to-SOC crosswalk that bridges College Scorecard academic programs to career outcomes.

record_id.description: A unique identifier for each row, computed automatically from the SOC code. This ID is stable — running the pipeline again on the same data produces the same ID for the same row. Format: 'ooh-' followed by 16 hexadecimal characters (e.g., 'ooh-a1b2c3d4e5f67890'). Useful for tracking specific occupation records across pipeline runs.

soc_code.description: The Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) code that uniquely identifies this occupation. SOC is the federal taxonomy used by BLS, O*NET, and the Census Bureau to classify all U.S. occupations. The format is XX-XXXX, where the first two digits identify the major occupation group (for example, 15 = Computer and Mathematical) and the last four digits identify the specific occupation (for example, 15-1252 = Software Developers). This is the natural key of the table and the primary join key for connecting BLS occupation data to College Scorecard programs via the CIP-to-SOC crosswalk, and to O*NET task-level data via direct SOC join.
soc_code.cde_rationale: Natural key and primary crosswalk anchor for the CIP-to-SOC bridge that links College Scorecard programs to career outcomes. Every downstream Gold zone join (ERN stat, GRW stat, market boss fight, ceiling boss fight) keys on soc_code. Also the join key for O*NET task-level data. Incorrect or missing values would sever the program-to-occupation linkage that is the core analytical capability of FutureProof.

occupation_title.description: The official name of the occupation as published by the Bureau of Labor Statistics. For example, 'Software developers' or 'Registered nurses.' Each SOC code maps to exactly one title. This is a human-readable label used for display in career guidance products — the soc_code field is the authoritative identifier for the occupation. Also used to derive the catchall_flag (occupations with 'all other' in the title).

soc_major_group.description: The 2-digit code identifying which of the 22 SOC major occupation groups this occupation belongs to. For example, '15' means Computer and Mathematical occupations, '29' means Healthcare Practitioners and Technical occupations. Derived by taking the first 2 characters of the SOC code. Useful for grouping occupations into broad career families and as a fallback when detailed crosswalk matches are unavailable.

soc_major_group_name.description: The human-readable name for the SOC major occupation group. For example, 'Management', 'Computer and Mathematical', or 'Healthcare Practitioners and Technical'. There are 22 valid group names, one for each major group code. Derived via lookup from the soc_major_group code against the standard SOC taxonomy.
soc_major_group_name.source_column: Derived via SOC 22-group taxonomy lookup on soc_major_group

broad_occupation_flag.description: A true/false flag identifying whether this occupation is a 'broad' or rolled-up category that aggregates multiple detailed occupations into one row. Exactly 7 occupations are flagged (13-1020, 13-2020, 29-2010, 31-1120, 39-7010, 47-4090, 51-2090). These broad codes need special handling in the CIP-to-SOC crosswalk and O*NET joins because they may map to multiple detailed occupations on the other side. Career guidance derived from broad codes should carry lower confidence than guidance from detailed codes.
broad_occupation_flag.source_column: Derived from soc_code (hardcoded 7-code list from Bronze SOC audit)

catchall_flag.description: A true/false flag identifying whether this occupation is an 'all other' residual category. These are legitimate BLS categories that represent the grab-bag of occupations not individually classified within a minor group — for example, 'Managers, all other' (11-9199) could include winery managers, laundromat managers, or any other manager not captured by a more specific SOC code. About 70 occupations carry this flag. Career guidance derived from catchall categories should carry lower confidence because the category is inherently heterogeneous.
catchall_flag.source_column: Derived from occupation_title (case-insensitive 'all other' substring match)

employment_current.description: The number of people currently employed in this occupation across the United States, as of the base year of the BLS projection cycle (2023). This value has already been converted from thousands to actual count in the Bronze zone (for example, a source value of 1795.5 thousand became 1,795,500). This gives a sense of the occupation's size in the U.S. labor market. Positive when present; null values are allowed defensively but none are expected in the current data.
employment_current.cde_rationale: Current employment count is a primary input to Gold zone employment statistics. Backs the baseline for employment change calculations and market size assessments. Required for the Market boss fight (current labor market size by occupation). Incorrect values would distort employment statistics presented to students making career decisions.
employment_current.source_column: raw.bls_ooh.employment_current (pass-through, already converted from thousands in Bronze)

employment_projected.description: The number of people projected to be employed in this occupation at the end of the BLS 10-year projection horizon (2034). Like employment_current, this value is already converted from thousands to actual count. Comparing this to employment_current shows whether BLS expects the occupation to grow or shrink. Positive when present; null values are allowed defensively but none are expected in the current data.
employment_projected.cde_rationale: Projected employment count at end of the 10-year BLS projection horizon. Together with employment_current, this enables the forward-looking employment change metrics that feed Gold zone GRW stats and Market boss fight. Students rely on projected employment to assess whether their target occupation is growing or shrinking.
employment_projected.source_column: raw.bls_ooh.employment_projected (pass-through, already converted from thousands in Bronze)

employment_change.description: The numeric change in employment between the base year and the projected year, in number of workers. A positive value means the occupation is expected to add jobs; a negative value means it is expected to lose jobs. This is the difference between projected and current employment. About 232 occupations (27.9%) show declining employment. Not independently critical since it can be recomputed from employment_projected minus employment_current.

employment_change_pct.description: The projected percentage change in employment over the BLS 10-year projection cycle. For example, a value of 16.7 means the occupation is expected to grow 16.7% over the projection period. Negative values indicate declining occupations — about 27.9% of occupations have negative growth. This is the most commonly cited BLS growth statistic and directly backs the GRW (growth) stat in Gold zone career outcomes. It also drives the growth_category derivation. Valid range: -50.0% to +60.0%.
employment_change_pct.cde_rationale: Percentage change in employment over the BLS 10-year projection cycle. Directly backs the GRW (growth) stat in Gold zone career outcomes. Drives growth_category derivation. This is the primary metric students see when evaluating whether an occupation is growing or declining. Incorrect values would misclassify occupation growth tiers and mislead career guidance.

openings_annual_avg.description: The estimated average number of job openings per year in this occupation over the projection period. This includes openings from both growth (new positions being created) and replacement (workers retiring or leaving the occupation). Even occupations that are shrinking overall still have annual openings because existing workers leave. This is a practical measure of how many entry points exist for someone pursuing this career. Non-negative; 4 tiny occupations have zero openings due to BLS rounding.

growth_category.description: A plain-English classification of how fast this occupation is growing or shrinking, based on the employment_change_pct value. Six categories: 'declining_fast' (shrinking more than 10%), 'declining' (shrinking 1-10%), 'stable' (changing less than 1% in either direction), 'growing' (growing 1-10%), 'growing_fast' (growing 10-20%), and 'booming' (growing 20% or more). These thresholds are based on BLS convention — average growth across all occupations is typically 3-5% over a 10-year cycle, so +/-1% is considered stable and 10%+ is notably strong. Null only when employment_change_pct is null (0 expected in current data).

median_annual_wage.description: The median annual wage for workers in this occupation, in U.S. dollars. 'Median' means half of workers earn more and half earn less. This is the primary compensation metric and directly backs the ERN (earnings) stat in Gold zone career outcomes. Null for 23 occupations where BLS does not report wage data (17 physicians/surgeons, 5 performers, 1 fishing/hunting worker). When present, values range from approximately $25,000 to $250,000. The max observed value is $238,380 — no wages are capped in the interactive export used as the source.
median_annual_wage.cde_rationale: Median annual wage is the primary compensation metric for occupations. Directly backs the ERN (earnings) stat in Gold zone career outcomes. This is the headline number students see when evaluating occupation earning potential. Feeds debt-to-earnings comparisons when combined with College Scorecard debt data via the CIP-SOC crosswalk. Null for 23 occupations where BLS does not report wage data. Incorrect values would directly mislead career earnings guidance.

median_wage_capped.description: A flag indicating whether the median wage for this occupation was top-coded (capped) by BLS at $239,200. When true, the actual median wage exceeds that amount but BLS does not report the exact figure. Currently 0 True values in the interactive export used as the data source — preserved as a defensive measure against future data source changes. Always false when median_annual_wage is null.

wage_available.description: A convenience flag that is true when the occupation has wage data available (median_annual_wage is not null) and false when it does not. This makes it easy to filter to occupations with wage data without checking median_annual_wage for null. Expected: 809 True (occupations with wage data) and 23 False (occupations without wage data).

education_typical.description: The typical level of education that most workers need to enter this occupation, as classified by BLS. This is the original human-readable label such as 'Bachelor's degree', 'High school diploma or equivalent', or 'Doctoral or professional degree'. BLS defines eight education levels. This is the entry-level requirement — some workers in the occupation may have more or less education. The normalized version of this field is education_level_name (derived from education_code).
education_typical.notes: Original BLS text label. No business term assigned — normalized version education_level_name (BT-039) is the governed field.

education_code.description: A numeric code representing the typical entry-level education requirement for this occupation, using the BLS education classification. The codes are: 1 = Doctoral or professional degree, 2 = Master's degree, 3 = Bachelor's degree, 4 = Associate's degree, 5 = Postsecondary nondegree award, 6 = Some college no degree, 7 = High school diploma or equivalent, 8 = No formal educational credential. IMPORTANT: This is a categorical code, not a number you should average or sum — the ordering represents decreasing education level, not a quantity.

education_level_name.description: The normalized human-readable label for the education requirement, derived from education_code via an 8-row lookup table. For example, code 3 maps to 'Bachelor's degree'. This provides consistent, standardized education level labels across all occupations. Null when education_code is null (0 expected in current data).

work_experience.description: The typical amount of work experience in a related occupation that is needed to enter this occupation, as classified by BLS. Values are: 'None' (no related experience needed), 'Less than 5 years', or '5 years or more'. This is the original BLS text label, passed through from the Bronze zone.
work_experience.notes: Original BLS text label. No business term assigned — coded version work_experience_code (BT-044) is the governed field.

work_experience_code.description: A numeric code representing the typical related work experience requirement for this occupation. The codes are: 1 = 5 years or more, 2 = Less than 5 years, 3 = None. IMPORTANT: This is a categorical code, not a number you should average or sum.

training_typical.description: The typical on-the-job training that most workers need to attain competency in this occupation, as classified by BLS. Values range from 'None' to specialized paths like 'Apprenticeship' or 'Internship/residency'. This is the original BLS text label, passed through from the Bronze zone.
training_typical.notes: Original BLS text label. No business term assigned — coded version training_code (BT-045) is the governed field.

training_code.description: A numeric code representing the typical on-the-job training requirement for this occupation. The codes are: 1 = Internship/residency, 2 = Apprenticeship, 3 = Long-term on-the-job training, 4 = Moderate-term on-the-job training, 5 = Short-term on-the-job training, 6 = None. IMPORTANT: This is a categorical code, not a number you should average or sum.

source_load_date.description: The date on which the source data was loaded into the pipeline's raw zone. This tells you when the data was fetched from the Bureau of Labor Statistics, not when BLS published the projections. Renamed from 'load_date' in the Bronze zone for clarity. Useful for tracking data freshness and identifying which load batch a row belongs to.
source_load_date.source_column: raw.bls_ooh.load_date (renamed to source_load_date, cast to DATE)

ingested_at.description: The exact date and time when this row was written to the Silver zone base table. This is generated automatically at transformation time and is used for pipeline auditing and tracking data freshness. It tells you when the Silver transformation ran, not when the source data was collected or published.

consumable.occupation_profiles.description: The occupation profiles data product for FutureProof. Each row represents one U.S. occupation identified by its SOC code, enriched with scored growth metrics (GRW score on a 1-10 scale), wage positioning (percentile ranks overall and within education tier), market opportunity scoring, and data quality context. This table answers 'What does this career look like, and where is it headed?' It powers the GRW and partial ERN pentagon stats, the Market and Ceiling boss fights, and Gemma's career descriptions. This Gold product stands alone as an occupation reference and does not require the CIP-to-SOC crosswalk — that connection happens in a separate cross-source Gold product.

record_id.description: A unique identifier for each row, computed automatically from the SOC code using a deterministic hash function. This ID is stable — running the pipeline again on the same data produces the same ID for the same row. Format: 'op-' followed by 16 hexadecimal characters. The 'op' prefix distinguishes Gold occupation profile records from Silver records (which use the 'ooh' prefix).

soc_code.description: The Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) code that uniquely identifies this occupation. Format is XX-XXXX, where the first two digits identify the major occupation group (for example, 15 = Computer and Mathematical) and the last four identify the specific occupation (for example, 15-1252 = Software Developers). This is the natural key of the table and the primary join key for connecting occupation data to College Scorecard programs (via the CIP-to-SOC crosswalk) and to O*NET task-level data.
soc_code.cde_rationale: Natural key and primary join anchor for the consumable occupation profiles table. Every downstream consumer queries by soc_code: Gemma agent lookups, CIP-to-SOC crosswalk joins, O*NET enrichment, pentagon stat rendering, boss fight engine, and compare-screen queries. Incorrect or missing values would sever the program-to-occupation linkage.

occupation_title.description: The official name of the occupation as published by the Bureau of Labor Statistics. For example, 'Software developers' or 'Registered nurses.' Each SOC code maps to exactly one title. This is the human-readable label that students interact with in career descriptions, the compare screen, and Gemma's guidance — while soc_code is the join key, this is the field end users actually see.
occupation_title.cde_rationale: Primary display label consumed directly by the Gemma agent for career descriptions, the FutureProof compare screen, and all user-facing occupation references. An incorrect title would misidentify careers in student-facing guidance.

soc_major_group.description: The 2-digit code identifying the broad occupation family this career belongs to. There are 22 major groups defined by the SOC system. For example, '15' means Computer and Mathematical occupations, '29' means Healthcare Practitioners. This code is derived from the first two characters of the SOC code and is used for grouping and filtering occupations by family.

soc_major_group_name.description: The human-readable name for the SOC major group. For example, 'Computer and Mathematical Occupations' for major group code 15, or 'Management Occupations' for code 11. Derived via lookup from the 2-digit SOC major group code.

broad_occupation_flag.description: A true/false flag indicating whether this occupation is a rolled-up 'broad' category that aggregates multiple detailed occupations into one row. There are exactly 7 broad occupations in this dataset (for example, SOC 13-1020 'Buyers and Purchasing Agents'). When true, the data represents a composite of multiple distinct jobs, so career guidance based on this row will be less specific. This flag is one of the inputs used to determine the confidence tier for each occupation.

catchall_flag.description: A true/false flag indicating whether this occupation is a residual 'all other' category — a catch-all for occupations that do not fit into more specific SOC codes within a group. There are approximately 70 catchall occupations (for example, '15-1299 Computer Occupations, All Other'). Like broad occupations, catchalls represent heterogeneous groups, so career guidance based on these rows will be less specific. This flag is one of the inputs used to determine the confidence tier.

employment_current.description: The current number of workers employed in this occupation across the United States. This is an actual headcount (not in thousands). For example, Registered Nurses has approximately 3.4 million. This field provides the employment base for growth projections and is consumed by Gemma for career descriptions ('this field employs X workers'). It is also one of the four core fields used to calculate the data completeness score.
employment_current.cde_rationale: Primary input to the Market boss fight and provides the baseline for employment change assessments. Directly consumed by Gemma for career descriptions and powers the market size component of career guidance. Incorrect values would distort employment statistics presented to students.

employment_projected.description: The projected number of workers that will be employed in this occupation at the end of the BLS 10-year projection horizon (currently projecting to 2034). This is an actual headcount. Combined with employment_current, it drives the employment_change_pct that feeds the GRW score. This field is not independently critical in the Gold layer because the derived grw_score and employment_change_pct carry the analytical signal to consumers.

employment_change_pct.description: The percentage change in employment for this occupation over the BLS 10-year projection cycle (currently 2024-2034). Positive values mean the occupation is growing; negative values mean it is declining. For example, a value of 15.8 means employment is projected to grow 15.8%. This is the primary input to the GRW score — the piecewise linear function converts this percentage into the 1-10 growth score. It also drives the growth_category classification. This is one of the four core fields used to calculate data completeness.
employment_change_pct.cde_rationale: Primary input to grw_score derivation that backs the GRW pentagon stat. Also drives growth_category and is a 60% input to market_score. Incorrect values corrupt the GRW score, growth_category, market_score, and all downstream growth-related guidance.

openings_annual_avg.description: The projected average number of job openings per year for this occupation, including both openings from growth (new positions) and from replacement (workers leaving the occupation). A high value means there are many opportunities to enter this career each year, regardless of whether the occupation is growing or shrinking overall. This field is an input to the market score — it carries 40% of the weight in the combined growth-plus-openings signal. It is one of the four core fields used to calculate data completeness.

growth_category.description: A plain-English category describing how fast this occupation is growing or declining. One of six values: 'declining_fast' (shrinking rapidly), 'declining' (shrinking), 'stable' (little change), 'growing' (modest growth), 'growing_fast' (strong growth), or 'booming' (exceptional growth). This category is derived from employment_change_pct in the Silver zone. Every row has a value because all 832 occupations have employment data.

grw_score.description: A score from 1.0 to 10.0 representing how strong this occupation's employment growth outlook is, relative to BLS benchmarks. This score directly backs the GRW (growth) stat on the FutureProof pentagon display. It is computed from employment_change_pct using a piecewise linear function: severe decline maps to 1.0, national average growth (~4%) maps to approximately 6.0, and exceptional growth (20%+) maps toward 10.0. Higher is better — a score of 8 means strong growth, while 3 means the occupation is shrinking. Currently non-null for all 832 rows.
grw_score.cde_rationale: Primary scored output backing the GRW pentagon stat. Consumed by the game engine for pentagon display and by Gemma for growth characterization. Also a 60% input to market_score. Incorrect values directly misrepresent occupation growth potential to students.
grw_score.source_column: Derived from base.bls_ooh.employment_change_pct via piecewise linear function

grw_score_rounded.description: The GRW score rounded to the nearest whole number, producing an integer from 1 to 10. This is the display-ready version of the GRW score used on the FutureProof pentagon and compare screen. For example, a grw_score of 6.47 becomes a grw_score_rounded of 6. Null only if grw_score is null (currently 0 rows).

median_annual_wage.description: The median annual wage for workers in this occupation, in U.S. dollars. This is the midpoint — half of workers earn more, half earn less. For example, Software Developers have a median annual wage of $133,080. This field is null for 23 occupations where BLS does not report wage data (typically because workers in those occupations are self-employed or have irregular compensation). This is the primary compensation metric backing the ERN (earnings) stat and is an input to both wage percentile rankings and the Ceiling boss fight.
median_annual_wage.cde_rationale: Primary compensation metric backing the ERN stat. Consumed by Gemma for salary narrative, Ceiling boss fight, debt-to-earnings comparisons via CIP-SOC crosswalk, and the compare screen. Input to wage_percentile_overall and wage_percentile_education_tier derivations. Incorrect values would mislead career earnings guidance and corrupt 4 downstream derived columns.

wage_available.description: A convenience flag that is true when median_annual_wage has a value and false when it is null. There are 809 occupations with wage data (true) and 23 without (false). This flag is used as an input to the confidence tier derivation — occupations without wage data are assigned 'low' confidence because the ERN stat cannot be computed for them.

wage_percentile_overall.description: A value from 0.0 to 1.0 showing where this occupation's median wage ranks compared to all other occupations that have wage data. A value of 0.85 means this occupation's median wage is higher than 85% of all occupations. Computed using PERCENT_RANK across 809 occupations with wage data (the 23 occupations without wage data are excluded from the ranking and have null values). This percentile is an input to the wage_tier derivation and is consumed by the compare screen for relative pay positioning.
wage_percentile_overall.cde_rationale: Directly powers the ERN stat relative positioning and the wage_tier derivation. Consumed by the compare screen to show how an occupation's pay ranks overall. Backs the Ceiling boss fight in combination with wage_percentile_education_tier. Incorrect values would misclassify wage tiers and misrepresent relative earning potential.
wage_percentile_overall.source_column: Derived: PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY median_annual_wage), null-safe (excludes null-wage rows)

wage_percentile_education_tier.description: A value from 0.0 to 1.0 showing where this occupation's median wage ranks compared to other occupations that require the same level of education. For example, if a Bachelor's-requiring occupation has a wage_percentile_education_tier of 0.90, it pays better than 90% of other occupations that also require a Bachelor's degree. This answers the question 'How well does this career pay relative to peers needing the same degree?' Null for the 23 occupations without wage data. This percentile directly backs the Ceiling boss fight.
wage_percentile_education_tier.cde_rationale: Directly backs the Ceiling boss fight — answers 'how close to the earnings ceiling am I within my education tier?' Consumed by the game engine and Gemma for education-adjusted compensation guidance. Incorrect values would give students a false impression of whether their career pays well for their degree level.
wage_percentile_education_tier.source_column: Derived: PERCENT_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY education_code ORDER BY median_annual_wage), null-safe

wage_tier.description: A plain-English category showing how this occupation's pay compares to all other occupations. One of five values: 'low' (bottom 25% of earners), 'below_average' (25th to 50th percentile), 'above_average' (50th to 75th percentile), 'high' (75th to 90th percentile), or 'very_high' (top 10% of occupations). Null for the 23 occupations without wage data. This is derived from wage_percentile_overall — the percentile carries the analytical precision, while the tier provides a quick human-readable summary.

education_code.description: A BLS code indicating the typical education level needed to enter this occupation. The codes range from 1 to 8: 1 = Doctoral or professional degree, 2 = Master's degree, 3 = Bachelor's degree, 4 = Associate's degree, 5 = Postsecondary nondegree award, 6 = Some college (no degree), 7 = High school diploma or equivalent, 8 = No formal educational credential. This is a categorical code, not a number you should average. It is used as the partition key for wage_percentile_education_tier (comparing pay within the same education requirement) and consumed by Gemma for 'what to study' guidance.

education_level_name.description: The human-readable label for the typical education level required to enter this occupation, such as 'Bachelor's degree' or 'High school diploma or equivalent'. This is derived from the education_code via a standard 8-row lookup table. Consumed by Gemma for career description display. Null when education_code is null.

work_experience_code.description: A BLS code indicating the typical work experience needed to enter this occupation. There are three values: 1 = 5 or more years, 2 = Less than 5 years, 3 = None. This is a categorical code, not a number you should average.

training_code.description: A BLS code indicating the typical on-the-job training needed to achieve competency in this occupation. Codes range from 1 to 6: 1 = Internship/residency, 2 = Apprenticeship, 3 = Long-term on-the-job training, 4 = Moderate-term on-the-job training, 5 = Short-term on-the-job training, 6 = None. This is a categorical code, not a number you should average.

market_score.description: A score from 1.0 to 10.0 representing the overall labor market opportunity for this occupation, combining both the growth trajectory and the volume of available openings. The formula is: 60% weight on the GRW score (growth direction) plus 40% weight on the openings score (how many job openings exist each year, ranked by percentile). This composite score backs the Market boss fight in FutureProof. A high market score means the occupation is both growing and has many openings — a strong labor market signal. Currently non-null for all 832 rows.
market_score.cde_rationale: Directly backs the Market boss fight in the FutureProof game engine. An incorrect market_score would directly miscalibrate the Market boss fight difficulty and mislead students about labor market opportunity.
market_score.source_column: Derived: 0.6 * grw_score + 0.4 * openings_score (openings_score from PERCENT_RANK on openings_annual_avg mapped to 1-10)
